narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates an 84% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—along with a low undergraduate GPA of 2.3, an LSAT score of 23.5, low family income, and your background as a Black male planning full-time work. Compared to averages, your LSAT is well below the usual 36.7, your undergrad GPA trails the standard 3.21, and your early grades rank at the bottom while most students hover around the fifth or sixth decile. The model weighed these factors carefully, with your LSAT score having the strongest pull toward predicting failure, followed closely by your low first-year decile ranking, then your third-year decile, your racial background, family income level, and undergrad GPA in that order of influence. Less impactful but still leaning toward failure were your plans for full-time work and being male, though these played smaller roles overall. Every one of these elements nudged the prediction higher toward bar exam struggles rather than success, painting a picture riskier than for typical applicants who pass. It's tough to hear, especially since demographics like race, gender, and family income aren't things you can change, but your academic metrics offer real paths forward. To boost your odds next time, prioritize retaking the LSAT with intensive prep to aim higher than 23.5, as it was the top driver here. Similarly, building a stronger academic record—perhaps through post-bac courses or grad-level work—could offset those low early deciles and GPA, showing upward momentum. Switching to part-time work might ease pressure too, though it's a minor factor. Ultimately, dedicated bar exam prep tailored to your learning style, drawing from your full-time commitment, could turn this around—many students with similar starts succeed by focusing on consistent study habits. Keep pushing; your determination is already a strength the model can't measure.
